focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

Cold filmed the inside of the windscreen before daylight had done much for the mews behind the Ashdowns' house. Beni sat with the engine off and his coat zipped to the throat, rubbing a clear patch in the glass with the side of his thumb while the stone stayed dark and the bins waited against the wall. It was a little before seven. The car looked expensive even under the weak security light. That was part of the trick. The black paint made a promise the rest of the day would keep cashing: no mess, no smell, no history, no one poor enough to be cold inside it.

The service door opened once for a delivery of flowers. White boxes came through in the arms of a man in a padded vest, then the door shut again and left behind a ribbon of warmer air carrying polish, radiator heat, and something meaty from downstairs. London at that hour smelled of wet stone and bus exhaust. The house smelled of polish over stock and radiator dust, and from downstairs came the sharp knock of a lid on cast iron.

He liked the first minutes of a job best, before anyone had started needing things. There was still sequence then: he picked up, drove, waited where he was told, kept the car clean, and kept his face neutral. The job got worse the more it asked you to stand three feet away with your face arranged until your toes went numb.

At seven ten Bea came down the mews steps by herself, moving fast because the rain had started again and the narrow gap between the houses funneled it straight at her face. She had her blazer on, skirt under a dark raincoat, and a school satchel that probably cost more than his monthly Oyster topped with a ridiculous plastic frog keyring, bright green and one-eyed, bumping against the polished leather. The frog should have looked childish. Instead it made...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e door."

"No. Tell me why you're here."

The line dropped so cleanly through the rain that Beni felt it in his own shoulders. Nina's free hand lifted a few inches from her side, palm turned up, not quite begging and not far enough from it.

"I need five minutes," she said. "You can despise me at floor level."

Jonas looked past her then, taking in the Mercedes, the dark windows, the driver still out in the weather. When he looked down again his face had closed further.

"Are you alone?"

"Yes."

He let her stand there another beat, long enough for the asking to stay visible. Then he disappeared from the walkway.

By the time Beni had wiped the heel print from the mat with one of Mercer's black cloths, the downstairs door had clicked open. Nina did not look back at the car before going in.

He got behind the wheel and pulled away slowly because the street was tight and because there was no reason now to show speed. In the mirror the warehouse receded into wet brick and white light. His route sheet still said Belgravia. The return leg to the depot would be dead mileage. He had another damp hour between himself and Tottenham. None of that was what sat with him.

He had been noticed. That was all.

She had not asked his name to thank him. She had asked because she wanted the next booking clean: the same driver at the side exit, the same shut mouth, the same hands to get the blood and city grit off Mercer's cream mat before the car went back out.

At the red light by the canal he touched the indicator and waited for it to change. Rain tapped the roof in that exact little place above his head. He looked at the blurred glow behind him where Jonas's studio still stood and knew what Mercer would call it by morning: `BY REQUEST`. That was not respect. It was a note for dispatch.
